authoritative_sources.title: Automated Clearinghouse Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Federal Reserve is an operator of ACH services and this official source is authoritative for ACH service structure, settlement posture, and terminology used in payment operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ACH payment intake, settlement posture, and posting readiness review

authoritative_sources.title: Operating Circular 4: Automated Clearing House Items
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Reserve Banks / Federal Reserve Financial Services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Operating Circular 4 is the Federal Reserve Banks' public operating rule for clearing and settlement of ACH credit and debit items and is authoritative for ACH workflow duties and exception boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ACH posting, return, and settlement handling

authoritative_sources.title: Fedwire Funds Service
authoritative_sources.publisher: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Federal Reserve operates the Fedwire Funds Service and is the authoritative public source for wire-processing terminology, participant roles, and finality-aware workflow framing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Wire payment intake, release, and repair handling

authoritative_sources.title: Operating Circular 6: Funds Transfers Through the Fedwire Funds Service
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Reserve Banks / Federal Reserve Financial Services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Operating Circular 6 and Regulation J define public duties and handling requirements for Fedwire funds transfers, making this source authoritative for wire exception, timing, and responsibility boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Wire exception review and funds-transfer handling

authoritative_sources.title: 12 CFR 1005.11 Procedures for Resolving Errors
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CFPB is authoritative for Regulation E implementation, and the rule text is directly relevant to error-resolution timing, evidence, and communication boundaries for disputed electronic payments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Payment dispute intake and consumer-impact handling

authoritative_sources.title: 12 CFR 1005.33 Procedures for Resolving Errors
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CFPB's remittance-transfer rule is authoritative for disclosure-sensitive consumer payment workflows where investigation, refund, or correction handling materially changes the answer.

authoritative_sources.title: Funds Transfers Recordkeeping
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council BSA/AML InfoBase
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FFIEC provides official interagency examination guidance used by U.S. banking regulators, making this source authoritative for transfer recordkeeping requirements and evidence sufficiency in funds-transfer handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Payment evidence validation and low-confidence review

authoritative_sources.title: Suspicious Activity Reporting
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council BSA/AML InfoBase
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FFIEC suspicious-activity reporting guidance is an official interagency examination reference and is authoritative for escalation boundaries, confidentiality posture, and refusal of unsupported fraud or AML conclusions.

authoritative_sources.title: RMS Manual of Examination Policies Section 4.2 Internal Routine and Controls
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDIC examination policy is authoritative for internal-control, segregation-of-duties, transaction-recording, and audit expectations in insured depository institutions, directly constraining payment posting and reconciliation handling.

authoritative_sources.title: Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures
authoritative_sources.publisher: Committee on Payments and Market Infrastructures and IOSCO / Bank for International Settlements
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CPMI-IOSCO PFMI is the recognized global standard for payment, clearing, and settlement system controls and is directly relevant to reconciliation, operational-risk, and finality-aware payments handling.

workflow_stages.name: payment intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm that the payment, account or policy, customer, product, approval, and retrieval prerequisites required to work the queue item are present before posting or exception handling begins.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: posting, billing, and source-support validation
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether remittance, transaction, approval, and customer records support a charge, posting, payment application, reversal, or billing outcome.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: reconciliation, denial, return, and suspense handling
workflow_stages.description: Compare payment-system activity, account or policy records, and general-ledger outcomes to identify matched items, denials, returns, suspense balances, unapplied cash, and unresolved variances.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: fraud, aml, and consumer-impact escalation
workflow_stages.description: Stop in-lane handling and route to the correct specialist when suspicious activity, AML-sensitive evidence, or disclosure-sensitive consumer impact changes the outcome.

workflow_stages.name: policy-conflict and authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate when public rules, tenant policy, or product-specific obligations conflict or when the request requires legal, accounting, underwriting, or approval authority outside operations.

artifacts: Account, policy, claim, transaction, invoice, and payment records
Remittance advice, payment-file records, ACH or wire details, return or denial indicators, and payment-hub activity
Approval histories, servicing notes, notices, disclosures, and customer communication logs
KYC, AML, fraud-monitoring, dispute, and suspicious-activity case artifacts
General-ledger extracts, reconciliation workpapers, suspense balances, unapplied-cash queues, and variance logs
Tenant policy extracts, approval matrices, product mappings, and jurisdiction-specific procedures
systems_of_record: policy administration or claims administration system
payment hub, ACH processor, wire platform, or lockbox processing system
case management, CRM, and document management systems
fraud monitoring and AML case systems
general ledger, reconciliation tooling, and regulatory reporting systems

allowed: Validate whether retrieved records support a charge, posting, payment application, reconciliation note, denial summary, or escalation outcome.
Prepare traceable billing or posting summaries, reconciliation notes, and exception packets with explicit uncertainty and owner routing.
Separate payment workflows when product line, jurisdiction, or tenant controls require different handling.
escalate: Fraud, AML, suspicious activity, or customer-harm indicators are present.
Consumer-protection obligations, remittance disclosures, or error-resolution rules may apply.
Account, policy, payment-system, or general-ledger records disagree.
A denial, dispute, write-off, or adjustment requires accounting, compliance, underwriting, or legal ownership.
refuse: Requests to create or alter payment, account, policy, or approval facts without source evidence.
Requests for regulated advice, legal interpretation, fraud or AML disposition, underwriting outcome, write-off approval, or accounting treatment.
Requests to flatten incompatible bank, payments, and insurance rules into one payment answer without tenant-approved mapping.

domain_failure_modes: Posting or adjusting a payment outcome that is not supported by authoritative remittance, transaction, approval, or account records
Treating a denied, returned, or suspicious payment as resolved in order to satisfy an operational deadline
Silently correcting reconciliation mismatches, suspense balances, or unapplied cash without traceable evidence and owner approval
Proceeding when fraud, AML, or consumer-impact signals require specialized review
Using generic finance language that hides payment-operations artifacts such as remittance advice, payment-hub events, return indicators, denial reasons, and suspense-account evidence

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Disclosure or reconciliation conflict that requires escalation
Missing documentation for posting or billing handling
Tenant-specific adaptation that preserves payment-lane boundaries

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Non-U.S. payment rails and card-network specific rules
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: The public baseline is U.S.-centric and centered on Federal Reserve, CFPB, FFIEC, FDIC, and CPMI-IOSCO sources. Tenant retrieval is required when card-network rules, correspondent-bank procedures, or non-U.S. rails materially change the workflow.

Insurance premium and claims-payment handling can vary by product and jurisdiction. The pack treats insurer-specific notice, disbursement, and approval rules as tenant-required facts before final handling.

notes: The source mix covers workflow, evidence, terminology, and failure handling rather than only one dimension.
No blocking ambiguity remains for validated packaging because tenant-specific product, jurisdiction, and network details are explicit prerequisites instead of hidden assumptions.
